questions.question: Define work in physics and give its SI unit.
questions.answer: Work is said to be done when a force is applied on a body and the body is displaced in the direction of the force. SI unit of work is joule (J).

questions.question: Derive the expression for work done when a constant force is applied along the direction of displacement.
questions.answer: Work done W = F × d × cosθ, where F is force, d is displacement, θ is angle between force and displacement. When force is along displacement, θ=0°, cos0°=1 → W=F×d.
answer_outline: Explain angle between force and displacement

questions.question: Differentiate between positive, negative and zero work with examples.
questions.answer: Positive work: force and displacement in same direction (pushing a cart forward). Negative work: force opposite to displacement (friction slowing a moving object). Zero work: displacement perpendicular to force (carrying a bag horizontally).

questions.question: Define kinetic energy and derive its expression.
questions.answer: Kinetic energy (KE) is the energy possessed by a body due to its motion. KE = 1/2 mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. Derived using work-energy theorem: W = ΔKE = 1/2 mv² - 0.

questions.question: Define potential energy and give its formula.
questions.answer: Potential energy (PE) is the energy possessed by a body due to its position or configuration. For a body of mass m at height h, PE = mgh.

questions.question: State and explain the law of conservation of energy.
questions.answer: Energy can neither be created nor destroyed, only transformed from one form to another. Total energy of an isolated system remains constant.

questions.question: A body of mass 2 kg is moving with a velocity of 3 m/s. Calculate its kinetic energy.
A body of mass 5 kg is lifted to a height of 10 m. Find its potential energy.
Explain the difference between conservative and non-conservative forces with examples.
questions.answer: Conservative forces: work done independent of path, depends only on initial and final position (e.g., gravity, spring). Non-conservative forces: work depends on path, energy dissipated as heat (e.g., friction, air resistance).

questions.question: A block slides down a frictionless incline of height 5 m. Find its speed at the bottom.
questions.answer: Using energy conservation: PE_top = KE_bottom → mgh = 1/2 mv² → v = √(2gh) = √(2×9.8×5) ≈ 9.9 m/s

questions.question: Define power and write its relation with work and time.
questions.answer: Power is the rate at which work is done. P = W / t, where W is work done and t is time.

questions.question: A person does 200 J of work in 20 s. Calculate his power.
Explain the difference between work, energy and power.
questions.answer: Work: transfer of energy via force. Energy: capacity to do work. Power: rate of doing work.

questions.question: Explain how a pendulum demonstrates the conversion between kinetic and potential energy.
questions.answer: At highest point: max PE, KE=0. At lowest point: max KE, PE=0. Energy continuously converts between KE and PE during swinging.

questions.question: State and explain the work-energy theorem.
questions.answer: Work done on a body is equal to the change in its kinetic energy: W = ΔKE = KE_final – KE_initial.

questions.question: A force of 10 N moves a body 5 m along the direction of force. Calculate work done.
Explain potential energy stored in a stretched spring and give formula.
questions.answer: Elastic potential energy: energy stored when spring is stretched/compressed. PE = 1/2 k x², k = spring constant, x = displacement from mean position.

questions.question: Explain how work can be zero even if a force is applied on a body.
questions.answer: If displacement is zero or perpendicular to force, work done W = F × d × cosθ = 0.

questions.question: A body of mass 3 kg is moving with speed 4 m/s. Calculate its kinetic energy and work required to stop it.
questions.answer: KE = 1/2 mv² = 1/2 × 3 × 16 = 24 J. Work required to stop = 24 J.
answer_outline: Apply W = ΔKE to stop body

questions.question: Explain different forms of energy with examples.
questions.answer: Mechanical energy (kinetic, potential), thermal energy (heat), chemical energy (battery, fuel), electrical energy, nuclear energy. Each can convert to others.

questions.question: Explain how efficiency of a machine is calculated.
questions.answer: Efficiency = (Useful work output / Total work input) × 100%

questions.question: A machine does 500 J of work using 600 J of energy input. Find its efficiency.
Explain how energy is conserved in a roller coaster ride.
questions.answer: At top: max PE, low KE; at bottom: max KE, low PE. Total mechanical energy remains constant (ignoring friction).

questions.question: Derive the relation between work done and change in potential energy when lifting a body vertically.
questions.answer: Work done W = F × h = m × g × h = ΔPE

questions.question: Explain how renewable and non-renewable energy sources affect work and energy usage in daily life.
questions.answer: Renewable sources (solar, wind) provide energy without depletion; non-renewable (coal, petrol) deplete resources. Efficient use affects work done in machines and energy availability.
